coredata.dc:title: Constructing 3D sub-micrometer CoO octahedrons packed with layered MoS2 shell for boosting photocatalytic overall water splitting activity
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Guo, Feng
Li, Mingyang
Shi, Yu
Shi, Minjie
Yan, Chao
coredata.dc:description: A 3D architectures of CoO@MoS2 composite photocatalyst was prepared by coating MoS2 onto the (1 1 1) facts of sub-micrometer CoO octahedrons via a facile solvothermal method. The CoO@MoS2 composites show a significantly improved photocatalytic activity for overall water splitting. At an optimal ratio of 3 wt% MoS2, CoO@MoS2-3% composite can split pure water with the stoichiometric ratio production of H2 and O2 under visible light irradiation, meanwhile, presents the highest H2 production evolution (1.07 μmol/h), which is almost four times than that of pure CoO (0.26 μmol/h). Moreover, the CoO@MoS2 composite still remains good stability after three successive cyclings (over 72 h). The enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability can be attributed that the layered MoS2 shell not only promoted the photo-induced charge transfer at the interface of CoO due to the sufficient contact area between CoO and MoS2, but also protected the exposed the (1 1 1) facts of CoO from devastation. Our work offers more insights into the development of a simple synthesis of well-shaped 3D-based composite photocatalysts towards the energy- and environmental-related applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CoO octahedrons
MoS2
Core-shell
Visible light
Overall water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CoO and CoO@MoS2 composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of CoO@MoS2 composite.
XRD patterns of as-prepared samples. (* mark shows the presence of MoS2).

TEM images of (a) pure CoO, (b) CoO@MoS2-1%, (c) CoO@MoS2-3% and (d) CoO@MoS2-5%. The insets are the TEM images of individual sample, respectively. (e) HAADF-STEM image and elemental mapping images of CoO@MoS2-3%, scale bar: 200 nm.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the CoO@MoS2-3% composite: (a) Co 2p (b) O 1s, (c) Mo 3d and (d) S 2p.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts.

H2/O2 evolutions from pure water with (a) CoO and (b) CoO@MoS2-3% as photocatalysts. (c) Gases evolutions of CoO@MoS2 composites with different contents of MoS2. (d) The stability of photocatalytic water splitting over CoO and CoO@MoS2-3%.
(a) PL spectra and (b) photocurrent response versus time of pristine CoO and CoO@MoS2-3%.
Schematic presentation of proposed photocatalytic overall water splitting mechanism for CoO@MoS2 core-shell photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Constructing 3D sub-micrometer CoO octahedrons packed with layered MoS2 shell for boosting photocatalytic overall water splitting activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A 3D architectures of CoO@MoS2 composite photocatalyst was prepared by coating MoS2 onto the (1 1 1) facts of sub-micrometer CoO octahedrons via a facile solvothermal method. The CoO@MoS2 composites show a significantly improved photocatalytic activity for overall water splitting. At an optimal ratio of 3 wt% MoS2, CoO@MoS2-3% composite can split pure water with the stoichiometric ratio production of H2 and O2 under visible light irradiation, meanwhile, presents the highest H2 production evolution (1.07 μmol/h), which is almost four times than that of pure CoO (0.26 μmol/h). Moreover, the CoO@MoS2 composite still remains good stability after three successive cyclings (over 72 h). The enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability can be attributed that the layered MoS2 shell not only promoted the photo-induced charge transfer at the interface of CoO due to the sufficient contact area between CoO and MoS2, but also protected the exposed the (1 1 1) facts of CoO from devastation. Our work offers more insights into the development of a simple synthesis of well-shaped 3D-based composite photocatalysts towards the energy- and environmental-related applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the consumption of the traditional energy and the aggravation of environmental contamination, it is imperative to develop renewable energy resources without emitting the carbon dioxide. Photocatalytic overall water splitting using particulate semiconductors is considered to be an appealing and economically feasible technology to relieve the energy crisis and environmental problems, which can directly convert solar energy into hydrogen [1–4]. In the past four decades, a larger number of semiconductors have been intensively investigated as photocatalysts to produce hydrogen from water splitting since the pioneering work by Honda and Fujishima in 1972 [5–9]. These photocatalysts, however, seldom exhibit satisfying photocatalytic H2 evolution activities without the help of cocatalysts even in the presence of sacrificial agents, which is mainly attributed to the fast recombination of photogenerated electron and holes. That is to say, loading cocatalysts onto the surface of semiconductor photocatalysts can take a significant effect in the production of H2 and O2 since they can not only provide more active sites for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction, but also promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of photocatalysts [10]. To date, most of the developed photocatalyst systems can utilize noble-metal-based cocatalysts (e.g. Ru [11], Rh [12], Pd [13,14], Pt [15], Au [16] and Ag [17]) to achieve high photocatalytic activity. Especially, Pt as a cocatalyst is always utilized owing to its largest work function and lowest overpotential for H2 evolution. Comparing with the rare and previous characteristics of noble-metal-based co-catalysts, the exploitation of noble-metal-free cocatalysts with high efficiency and low cost as a substitute for noble-metal cocatalysts to realize high efficient photocatalytic H2 production is more attractive from the viewpoint of large-scale commercialization.

Recent years, molybdenum disulphide (MoS2) with a layered structure co-catalyst has drawn tremendously scientific attention due to its high activity, earth-abundant composition, low cost and robustness [18]. The structure of MoS2 consists of three atom layers (S-Mo-S) stacking together via weak van der Waals interaction [19,20]. Based on the density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the exposed edges S atoms can form strong bonds with H+ in solution, which makes the protons easily reduced to H2 [21,22]. And the activity of MoS2 for hydrogen evolving reactions (HER) is increased because of the existence of abundant active sites stemming from the sulfur edge [23]. Thus, MoS2 is considered as the most potential alternative to Pt for H2 production [24]. It is demonstrated that using MoS2 as a cocatalyst to couple with other semiconductors can exhibit superior photocatalytic activity than the single semiconductor photocatalysts. For instance, Liu et al. reported that when MoS2 as a co-catalyst loading on CdS, the photocatalytic activity of H2 evolution of MoS2/CdS composite is increased by up to 39 times than that of pure CdS [25]. The MoS2 and graphene as cocatalysts on TiO2 composite has been synthetized by the Xiang’s group, which the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity is also enormously enhanced than that of pure TiO2 [26].

Recently, cobalt monoxide (CoO) octahedrons with exposed (1 1 1) facets has been reported to achieve stoichiometric ratio production of H2 and O2 for pure water splitting under visible-light irradiation [27]. However, the photocatalytic activity and stability of octahedral CoO leave much to be desired. One is the poor capability to separate electron-hole pairs and the other is the (1 1 1) facet usually and easily deactivated during the photocatalytic process caused by its polar catastrophe. Consequently, it remains an extremely challenging task to stabilize (1 1 1) facets of CoO octahedrons and further enhance its photocatalytic performance. Considering that a core-shell structure has multiple features of optimized interfacial contacts and corrosion protection, the construction of octahedral CoO-based core-shell hybrid has been high desirable.

In this work, 3D core-shell structured CoO@MoS2 composite photocatalyst was synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. Interestingly, the MoS2 shell thickness of 50–200 nm in the core-shell structure can be easily tuned by varying the concentration of MoS2 precursor during the solvothermal process. The formation procedure of CoO@MoS2 heterojunction was illustrated in Fig. 1 . Benefiting from the unique structural features, the photocatalytic performance of as-obtained CoO@MoS2 composite was tested by the photocatalytic overall water splitting under visible irradiation. The photocatalytic mechanism of CoO@MoS2 composite was also discussed in detail.

Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O, MoS2 and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and were of analytical reagent grade. The n-octanol was purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CoO and CoO@MoS2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 1.84 g of Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O was added to a mixed solvent containing 64 mL n-octanol and 16 mL ethanol and stirred for 2 h. Then, the homogeneous precursor was transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and kept at 220 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed with ethanol three times and finally dried at 70 °C in an oven. For the CoO@MoS2 composites, different weight percentages (1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt%) MoS2 were added to the fabricated process of CoO. After cooling to room temperature, the form powders were washed, dried and labelled as CoO@MoS2-1%, CoO@MoS2-3% and CoO@MoS2-5%.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of materials were recorded using an X‘Pert-ProMPD (Holand) D/max-γA X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images was taken on FEI-quanta 200 with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-SEM) were performed with a FEI-Tecnai F20 microscope operating at 200 kV, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Al Kα X-ray source. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Lambda 750 (Perking Elmer) spectrophotometer in the tested range of 200–800 nm. The photocurrent-time (i-t) curves were performed on a CHI 920C workstation (CH Instruments, Chenhua, Shanghai, China) using a standard three-electrode cell with a platinum wire as reference electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as counter electrode. The i-t curves were gained at open circuit potential in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte and a 300 W Xe lamp with UV-cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) were used as the visible light source. The EIS was recorded by using an alternating voltage of 5 mV amplitude in the frequency range of 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore structures were characterized by a Micromeritics ASAP-2050 porosimeter.

The photocatalytic water splitting tests were carried out in PCX-50C multichannel photochemical reaction device (Beijing Perfectlight Co. Ltd., China). The photocatalytic reaction was performed in 20 mL water suspension of photocatalyst (50 mg). Then the system was degassed to completely remove air, and irradiated with a white light-emitting diode (LED) as the visible light source (λ > 400 nm) from the bottom of the reactor system. An online GC-7900 gas chromatograph (GC) setup with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and 5 Å molecular sieve columns was employed for evolved gas detection.

XRD was employed to analyze the crystallographic structures and phase purities of the as-synthesized CoO and CoO@MoS2 photocatalyst. The black line in Fig. 2 is the pattern of pristine CoO. All the diffraction peaks of pure CoO can be indexed to the standard peaks of the face-centered cubic phase CoO (JCPDS No. 71-1178) [28]. The XRD patterns of CoO@MoS2 composite photocatalyst with different MoS2 content in Fig. 2 shows that the intensities of the diffraction peaks of MoS2 from the CoO@MoS2 composites are gradually strengthened with increasing the content of MoS2 (1–5 wt%). In addition, although MoS2 combined with CoO to form CoO@MoS2 composite, it is observed that the diffraction peaks of the composites are mainly similar to that of pure CoO, implying the introduction of MoS2 has no effects on the crystalline structure of CoO. And No other impurity peaks could be detected from the XRD measurements in the CoO@MoS2 composites, proving the successful formation of CoO@MoS2 composites.

The morphology and structure of as-prepared CoO and CoO@MoS2 samples are observed by using SEM and TEM, and shown in Fig. 3 . The SEM image reveals that pure CoO particles have an overall octahedral morphology with smooth surface, and the sizes of pure CoO are mainly about 400 nm (Fig. S1a and b). The TEM in Fig. 3a shows that as-synthesized CoO consisted of a well-defined octahedral structure, and the image of a single CoO is further shown in inset of Fig. 3a. TEM image (Fig. S2) clearly exhibits that pure MoS2 possesses obvious ripples and corrugations with a small thickness of around 6–8 nm and the uniform lateral size of 800 nm. the TEM images of CoO@MoS2 are demonstrated in Fig. 3b–d, the contrast between dark center and gray edge with the thickness of around 50–200 nm is clearly observed, implying the construction of core-shell structure of the composite. Importantly, the increasing loading amount of MoS2 leads to the construction of thicker MoS2 shell on the surfaces of CoO. As the loading contents of MoS2 are 1 wt%, 3 wt% and 5 wt%, the thickness of corresponding MoS2 shell on the core CoO are 50, 80 and 200 nm, respectively. Furthermore, elemental mapping was performed using the TEM coupled with STEM-EDX to confirm the elements formation and the structure of CoO@MoS2 composites. As shown in Fig. S3, the Co, O, Mo, and S elements are detected in the CoO@MoS2 composites. From the top views of a single particle of CoO@MoS2-3%, it can be seen that MoS2 nanosheets are uniformly distributed on the surface of octahedral CoO with exposed (1 1 1) facets, leading to a MoS2 shell around the CoO core, which favors the photo-generated electrons transfer due to the closed interfacial contact (Fig. 3e).

The surface chemical composition and chemical status of elements in the CoO@MoS2-3% composite were analyzed by XPS technique (Fig. 4 ). And Fig. S4 exhibits the XPS survey spectrum of CoO@MoS2-3% composite, which is easily observed the photoelectron peaks of Mo, S, Co and O elements. The Co 2p XPS spectrum in Fig. 4a shows two prominent peaks centered at 780.6 and 796.5 eV with another two satellite peaks at 786.5 and 802.9 eV, proving that Co exists as Co2+ in CoO [27]. As displayed in Fig. 4b, the O 1s spectrum can be fitted into two peaks at 529.4 and 531.1 eV, well-matching the lattice oxygen and chemisorbed and dissociated oxygen, respectively [29]. For the Mo 3d (Fig. 4c), the spectrum can be fitted into four peaks at 226.2, 229.1, 232.3 and 235.5 eV, respectively. One of those located at 226.2 eV correspond to S2- in the MoS2. Two conspicuous peaks (229.1 and 232.3 eV) are attributed to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively. Another peak at binding energy 235.5 eV is ascribed to Mo6+ for Mo-O [30], which might be attributed that the exposed Mo atoms are combined with O atoms in CoO. Fig. 4d shows two obvious characteristic peaks at 161.2 and 162.4, which are corresponded to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively.

Optical properties of CoO and CoO@MoS2 composites were characterized by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). In Fig. 5 , the band gap absorption edge of octahedral CoO (black line) shows light absorption over the entire wavelength range with two obvious adsorption edges which is in agreement with the previous report [31]. The bandgap of CoO is 2.51 eV according to the Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. S5), therefore it can only adsorb the light in the visible region. Notably, the light harvesting capacity become stronger owing to the loading of MoS2 on the surface of CoO octahedrons, which is more beneficial to make use of solar energy to produce photo-induced electron-hole pairs, further improving the photocatalytic activity of CoO. It is generally accepted that the band potentials play a key role in photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, the VB and CB band potentials of as-synthesized nanostructures were measured by combining corresponding diffuse reflectance spectra via the Kubelka-Munk function and Mott-Schottky plots. As shown in Fig. S6a, the band energy gap of CoO@MoS2-3% is 2.33 eV. Moreover, based on the Mott-Schottky measurement, the flat-band potentials of CoO@MoS2-3% is 1.71 V vs. NHE. It is well known that the flat-band potential is 0.1–0.3 eV lower than the valence band potential in semiconductor with the negative slope of the linear plot [32]. Consequently, the VB potential for CoO@MoS2-3% is 1.81 eV. Furthermore, the CB of CoO@MoS2-3% was calculated to be −0.52 eV based on its band gap (Fig. S6b). According to the above mentioned analysis results, its band alignments satisfy the necessary requirement for the production of photo-induced charges for photocatalytic overall water splitting under visible-light (Fig. S6c).

The photocatalytic performances for overall water splitting of as-prepared samples under visible-light irradiation (λ > 400 nm) are shown in Fig. 6 . It is noticeable that no gas can be obtained without light or catalysts when other conditions keep constant, which explains that the produced H2 in this work can be regarded as a photocatalytic reaction. Under visible-light irradiation, the evolutions of stoichiometric H2 and O2 from pure water by bare CoO and CoO@MoS2-3% linearly increase over time (Fig. 6a and b), respectively. However, pure CoO exhibits quite low photocatalytic H2 production rate owing to its fast recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Fig. 6c shows the photocatalytic water-splitting activities of the CoO@MoS2 composites. Also of note is that with increasing the amount of MoS2 (1–5 wt%) on the surface of CoO, a promising and synergetic improvement in the photocatalytic H2 and O2 evolutions of CoO@MoS2 composites are obtained (Fig. 6c), suggesting the fast interfacial electron transfer between CoO and MoS2 for enhanced the photocatalytic activity. And the MoS2 content played a significant role in the photocatalytic H2 production efficiency. Especially, when the amount of MoS2 reached 3 wt%, the H2 production rate of CoO@MoS2-3% achieved a maximum (1.07 μmol/h), which exceeded that of CoO by more than 4 times. But, after loading the excessive MoS2 content (>3%), the photocatalytic activity of CoO@MoS2 composite led to a slight decrease. This phenomenon could be attributed to the following reasons: (i) Redundant MoS2 leading to the thicker shell could hinder CoO absorbing light, which weakened the interaction between CoO and MoS2 and impede the transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes; (ii) Excessive MoS2 may reduce active site exposure, thus decreasing the photocatalytic activity [33]. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of as-prepared CoO@MoS2-3% photocatalyst was calculated to be 0.67% (see the supporting information). Moreover, the optimum H2 evolution was explored through adding CoO@MoS2-3% catalyst with different amounts into 20 mL pure water (Fig. S7). It is worth mentioning that with increasing the amount of catalyst, the H2 evolution rate also increased. When the amount increased to 10 mg in the 20 mL pure water (catalyst/water = 0.5), CoO@MoS2-3% catalyst presented the highest photocatalytic activity with the H2 evolution rate of 1.15 μmol/h, and then stayed almost the similar H2 evolution rate upon further addition. Generally, the utilization of sacrificial reagent in most photocatalytic systems is beneficial to enhancing the charge separation and improving the photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst. It is worthwhile mentioning that when the triethanolamine (TEOA) as a sacrificial agent had been injected into the reaction system (Fig. S8), the H2 evolution of CoO@MoS2-3% was obviously increased from 25.6 µmol to 77.4 µmol with the optimum solvent volume ratio (10 vol%) under visible light irradiation after 24 h. To fully assess the synthesized nanostructures for practical applications, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate was further measured at ambient temperature and pressure conditions. As shown in Fig. S9, although during the aerobic condition, as-prepared MoS2@CoO-3% still maintains the efficient activity for photocatalytic overall water splitting. Furthermore, it is necessary to investigate the photocatalytic durability of the synthetized photocatalysts, which is as important as activity from the practical application. In Fig. 6d, the average H2 and O2 evolution rates of CoO@MoS2-3% still remained steady after three cycles (over 72 h), indicating that CoO@MoS2 composite had good stability for photocatalytic H2 evolution. Meanwhile, after the cycling experiments, the diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns (Fig. S10) of CoO@MoS2-3% are not obviously changed, demonstrating that both structure and composition of as-prepared photocatalyst remain unaltered.

To explore the originals of the enhanced photocatalytic H2 performance of CoO@MoS2 composite, photoluminescence (PL) and photocurrent responses measurements have been carried out (Fig. 7 ). Generally, the PL emission signal comes from the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in a semiconductor, thus the higher PL intensity indicates the faster recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes [34–38]. As shown in Fig. 7a, the PL spectra of CoO and CoO@MoS2 were measured at the 325 nm excitation. The CoO has a strong peak at 460 nm in the PL spectrum, which is attributed to the band gap recombination of electron-hole pairs. Significantly, the PL emission intensity of the CoO sample clearly decreases after loading MoS2, implying that the introduced MoS2 can efficiently restrict the charge recombination in CoO. Moreover, Fig. 7b shows that the periodic on/off transient photocurrent measurements were further performed to confirm the above PL analysis, which can directly reflect the migration and transfer efficiency of the photoinduced carriers [39–42]. The higher the photocurrent of the sample is, the better the efficiency of the electrons and holes separation will be [43]. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the generated photocurrent intensity by CoO@MoS2 under the same conditions is much higher than that of CoO, demonstrating the strong ability of charge transfer and favorable recombination restraint in the CoO@MoS2 composite. In addition, the porosity and surface area of CoO and CoO@MoS2 composite were investigated through the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms method. As shown in Fig. S11, a typical type IV isotherm featuring a pronounced H3-type hysteresis loop on the basis of IUPAC classification indicated the presence of micropores and mesoporous within the CoO@MoS2 composites [44]. The SBET of CoO@MoS2-3% is 9.04 m2 g−1, which is much higher than that of CoO (0.56 m2 g−1). From the above datas, we can see that he introduction of MoS2 could obviously enhance the surface areas of CoO, providing more reaction active sites to further improve the photocatalytic activity.

On the basis of above discussion results, a tentative photocatalytic water splitting mechanism is presented for the CoO@MoS2 core-shell composite. As shown in Fig. 8 , under the visible light irradiation, pure CoO octahedrons could be excited to produce the electron-hole pairs. However, the photogenerated charge carriers quickly recombined in pure CoO without decorating co-catalyst, resulting in a quite low photocatalytic activity. After coating MoS2 shell onto the surface of CoO octahedrons, existing a strong thermodynamic driving force impels the photogenerated electrons in conduction band of CoO octahedrons core to easily transfer to MoS2 shell. Owing to the fact that the unsaturated active S atoms on exposed edges of MoS2 has strong bond with H+ in solution, and thus the trapped electrons at MoS2 are easily reduced to H2 [45], further improving the photocatalytic activity of pure CoO. Simultaneously, the photo-excited holes in the VB of CoO combined with H2O to produce O2. Additionally, the exposed (1 1 1) facets of CoO octahedrons can be preserved by MoS2 shell because of its favorable chemical stability and thermal conductivity, which prolongs the photocatalytic activity of CoO [27].

In summary, 3D core-shell architecture of CoO@MoS2 composites with different contents of MoS2 were successfully prepared by a facile solvothermal method for direct photocatalytic water splitting into H2 and O2 without using any sacrificial agents. The result shows that CoO@MoS2-5% composite can effectively split pure water under light irradiation, possessing the highest photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 1.07 μmol/h and excellent reusability (over 72 h). Such a high activity is around four times higher than that of pure CoO. This improved photocatalytic activity and stability of CoO@MoS2 composite can be attributed to the following three reasons: (i) 2D layered MoS2 nanosheet can provide a great number of active sites to more effectively take participate in photocatalytic reaction; (ii) Such a 3D hierarchical structure which consists of a core of CoO octahedrons and the shell of MoS2 nanosheets is propitious to enlarging intimate contact interface to promote the fast interfacial charge separation; (iii) MoS2 nanosheets can also improve the stability of octahedral CoO owing to its favorable chemical stability and thermal conductivity, which can protect the exposed (1 1 1) facets of CoO from devastation in photocatalysis. Our work may provide a significant insight into using noble-metal-free MoS2 as cocatalyst to construct the hetero-superstructure photocatalysts for the application in both solar energy conversion and environmental remediation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly acknowledge the funding for this project through the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 51873083), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering (Sichuan University) (No. sklpme2018-4-27), the Six Talent Peaks Project in Jiangsu Province (No. 2015-XCL-028), the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_1831, SJCX18_0759 and KYCX18_2340) and the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) (1062931806 and 1142931803).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.12.247.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
